full_response:
Let   

 L = {l1 , … , ln } , li = (xi , yi ) ∈ ℝ^2 , all xi ’s distinct and all yi ’s distinct.  

Goal: decide whether ∃ i ≠ j such that ‖li  – lj ‖2  < 1.

Because the threshold 1 is a constant, the task is a yes / no version of the classical
closest–pair problem.  
We reproduce that divide-and-conquer algorithm, adapting the proof to the fixed
distance 1.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
1 High-level idea
--------------------------------------------------------------------
1.  Sort the points once by x–coordinate.  
2.  Recursively split the set by a vertical line through the median x.
3.  If either side contains a forbidden pair we are done.
4.  Otherwise any violating pair must straddle the dividing line and each
    point then lies within one unit of the line.
    Because no two points are closer than one unit inside each half,
    at most seven points can lie in a 1 × 2 rectangle; hence every point
    needs to be compared with at most seven other points → O(n) work per level.

That gives the recurrence T(n)=2T(n/2)+Θ(n) ⇒ T(n)=Θ(n log n).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2 Auxiliary geometric fact
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Lemma 1.  
Let S be a set of points in the vertical strip

 Strip = { (x, y) | |x – xm| < 1 }

such that no two points in S are closer than 1
(we already know each side of the recursion is “clean”).
Then when the points of S are sorted by y–coordinate,
every point needs to be compared with at most the next seven points.

Proof.  
Partition the strip into rectangles of width 1 and height 1/2.
Any circle of radius 1 centered at a point p
intersects at most the two rectangles that touch p vertically
and at most four rectangles horizontally (because the strip is 2 units wide),
so at most eight rectangles total.  
Since each rectangle can contain at most one point
(otherwise two points would be ≤ √{(1)^2+(½)^2} < 1 apart),
the circle holds at most seven other points besides p. ∎

(The classical closest-pair proof gives the same bound 7.)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
3 Algorithm
--------------------------------------------------------------------
We maintain two arrays throughout every recursive call:

  Px : the points sorted by x-coordinate  
  Py : the same points sorted by y-coordinate.

Copying from parent to child can be done in Θ(n) per level by a linear scan.

Pseudocode
----------------------------------------------------------------
Closest-Within-One(L)
  sort L by x → Px
  sort L by y → Py
  return Recurse(Px, Py)

Recurse(Px, Py)          // |Px| = |Py| = m
  if m ≤ 3 then
       brute-force all ⎛m⎞₂ pairs
       return true if a pair is <1 apart else false

  let xm be x-coordinate of median point in Px
  split Px into PxL , PxR of size ⌈m/2⌉ and ⌊m/2⌋

  PyL ← [];  PyR ← []
  for p in Py                    // Θ(m)
        if p.x ≤ xm  then append p to PyL
        else               append p to PyR

  if Recurse(PxL, PyL) then return true
  if Recurse(PxR, PyR) then return true

  // cross pairs
  S ← [ p in Py  with |p.x – xm| < 1 ]   // already y-sorted
  for i = 0 .. |S|-1
        for j = i+1 .. min(i+7, |S|-1)   // Lemma 1
              if (S[j].y – S[i].y) ≥ 1   // optional early exit
                       break
              if distance(S[i], S[j]) < 1   return true

  return false
--------------------------------------------------------------------

--------------------------------------------------------------------
4 Correctness proof
--------------------------------------------------------------------
We prove by induction on the size m of the subproblem handled by Recurse.

Induction hypothesis H(m):  
Recurse(Px,Py) returns TRUE  iff  the corresponding set P
contains a pair of points whose Euclidean distance is <1.

Base m≤3.  The algorithm brute-forces all pairs, so H(m) holds.

Inductive step.  Assume H(k) holds for all k<m, let |P|=m.

• If a violating pair lies entirely in the left half,
  the first recursive call (size ≤ ⌈m/2⌉ < m) finds it by IH.  
  The algorithm then returns TRUE.

• Likewise for the right half.

• Otherwise every pair with distance <1 must have one endpoint in
  each half, hence both endpoints belong to the strip |x–xm|<1.
  The inner double loop enumerates
  every such pair because S contains exactly the strip’s points
  and, by Lemma 1, every pair at distance <1
  appears among at most the next seven successors in y-order.
  If such a pair exists we return TRUE, else FALSE.

Thus Recurse is correct; so is Closest-Within-One. ∎

--------------------------------------------------------------------
5 Running-time analysis
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Let T(m) be the worst-case time of Recurse on m points (excluding
the initial sorts which cost Θ(n log n)).

T(m) = 2T(m/2) + Θ(m) for m > 3, T(3)=Θ(1).

By the Master Theorem (a =2, b =2, f(m)=Θ(m)=Θ(m^{log_b a})),
T(m) = Θ(m log m).

Adding the initial two sorts gives Θ(n log n) total time.

Space: we store the two arrays per level plus recursion stack,
O(n) auxiliary space.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
6 Final result
--------------------------------------------------------------------
We can decide in Θ(n log n) worst-case time and Θ(n) space
whether any two among n launch-sites are closer than one unit, by the
divide-and-conquer algorithm above.

\boxed{\text{An }O(n\log n)\text{-time, }O(n)\text{-space divide-and-conquer algorithm detects whether } \exists\, l_i,l_j:\|l_i-l_j\|<1.} 

Confidence: 0.83
